title: Alleviating Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models with Active Retrieval Augmentation
methodology: The authors propose ARA (Active Retrieval-Augmented LVLM), a training-free framework that combines coarse-to-fine hierarchical retrieval (full-image plus object-region retrieval via Grounding DINO and CLIP), a CLIP-based reranking step over external image-caption databases (COCO and Visual Genome), and an active retrieval trigger based on mutual-information difficulty metrics (confidence-, query-, or image-aware) so retrieval only fires when the LVLM is uncertain. Retrieved coarse and fine pairs are then fused via probability-level or instance-level joint decoding to produce the final answer.
key_findings: Naively applying LLM-style retrieval augmentation to LVLMs can sometimes worsen hallucinations, motivating a more careful design.
Decomposing images into hierarchical retrieval targets (full image + grounded object regions) yields more relevant retrieved knowledge than full-image-only retrieval.
Selectively triggering retrieval only when the model has low certainty (via mutual-information-based query-aware metric) reduces unnecessary retrieval while still mitigating hallucinations.
Reranking retrieved pairs using CLIP text similarity to a LVLM-generated caption filters out visually similar but semantically wrong candidates.

ARA consistently improves three LVLMs (LLaVA-1.5, Qwen-VL, mPLUG-Owl2) across four hallucination benchmarks (POPE, MME hallucination subset, MMStar, MMBench), with F1 gains on POPE driven largely by recall improvements (up to ~10 points for mPLUG-Owl2) and ~8.8 average-point gains for LLaVA-1.5 on MMStar.
Improvements span object-level, attribute-level (especially color), and relation-level hallucinations.
limitations: The method is evaluated only on vision-language models, not on text-only LLM hallucination; transferability of insights to pure LLM RAG is implicit rather than tested.
Relies on multiple external components (CLIP, LLaMA2-7B for entity extraction, Grounding DINO), increasing system complexity and potential failure modes when grounding fails.
Retrieval databases are limited to COCO and Visual Genome, which may constrain generalization to domains outside common objects.
Several hyperparameters (fusion weight alpha, active-retrieval threshold) are set via grid search per benchmark, raising questions about robustness in deployment.
Token-level confidence triggering from prior LLM work is reported as not directly applicable to LVLMs, but the proposed alternatives still require careful threshold tuning.
relevance_reasoning: The paper directly addresses retrieval augmentation for hallucination reduction, but specifically in large vision-language models rather than general LLMs. Its core mechanisms (active triggering, hierarchical retrieval, reranking, joint decoding) offer transferable insights for LLM RAG, yet the empirical evidence and design choices are multimodal-specific, so it only partially answers a question framed about LLMs.
